text: IDIOPATHIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA Management Guidelines Emergency Department Princess Margaret Hospital for Children Perth, Western Australia Last reviewed: December 2005 Page 1 of 2 Dr Gary Geelhoed ITP is a common disorder in children, presenting with bruising, mucosal bleeding and petechiae. The condition is the result of thrombocytopenia caused by platelet auto-antibodies, often precipitated by intercurrent viral infections. Patients fall into two categories: (1) Acute ITP – approximately 90%. These patients present acutely with spontaneous bleeding and usually resolve within weeks or months of diagnosis. (2) Chronic ITP – ITP lasting more than 6 months - approximately 10%. Presentation may be more insidious and the condition often persists beyond 12 months. Most children present with petechiae and bruising, but there may be mucosal bleeding and epistaxis, although rarely rectal bleeding or haematuria. The risk of intracranial haemorrhage is <1%. This risk seems to be greatest early on in the disease. Intracranial bleeds are more likely to occur if the platelet count is < 10 x 109/L, especially if there is active mucosal bleeding or retinal haemorrhage. A “safe” platelet count is usually regarded as > 20 x 109/L, although if there is trauma or intracranial pathology even this level could be dangerous. Important elements in the history and examination of a child with suspected ITP History: • Bleeding symptoms: - Type, severity and duration of bleeding. - Haemostasis with previous invasive procedures. • Systemic symptoms, especially of recent (within 6 weeks) viral infection or exposure to viral infection. • History of recurrent infections suggesting immunodeficiency. • Recent live virus vaccination. • Medications - which may cause thrombocytopenia - which may exacerbate bleeding (eg. aspirin, warfarin) • Family history of thrombocytopenia or other haematological disorder. • Co-morbid conditions which may increase the risk of bleeding. • Lifestyle factors which may pose a risk for trauma and bleeding. Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura DPMS No 3531 Page 1 Examination: • Bleeding signs: - Type and location of bleeding (including retinal haemorrhages) • Pallor • Lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly • Evidence of infection • Dysmorphic features suggesting a congenital disorder or syndrome (eg. Fanconi syndrome) • Thrombocytopenia-absent radius (TAR) syndrome The diagnosis of ITP is made when the history, physical examination, full blood count and peripheral blood smear are all typical of ITP, and no abnormalities suggesting a different aetiology exist. Bone marrow examination is unnecessary in these patients Diagnosis History and examination excludes other causes of thrombocytopenia. Blood film shows only thrombocytopenia, with normal Hb and WCC (there may be some atypical lymphocytes). Film diagnosis is aided by the presence of large (left-shifted) platelets. Management The natural history of this disorder is that most patients will spontaneously remit within 6 months. Treatments are available including steroids and intravenous gammaglobulin which do not influence the natural history of the disorder but may raise the platelet count in severe forms of the disorder. The decision whether to treat or not should be made by the managing paediatric consultant. (Platelet transfusions are of no value in the management of ITP, but will be given in conjunction with IV gammaglobulin and high-dose steroids in the rare event of massive bleeding. Splenectomy is a last resort in rare cases). Refer to a Clinical Haematologist for bone marrow examination (BMA) if diagnosis is uncertain, atypical blood film features or haematological parameters, chronic ITP or failure to respond to therapy. Indications for Hospitalisation These include: (a) Any active bleeding, irrespective of the platelet count. (b) Any suspicion of intracranial haemorrhage requires urgent and active therapy. (c) A child with a platelet count of <10 x 109/L is at greater risk of spontaneous bleeding and should be assessed carefully for possible admission. (d) If avoidance of trauma at home cannot be assured, then rather admit. Most patients with a platelet count of >20 x 109/L will not require treatment and can be managed as outpatients. Check platelet count weekly. Patient should avoid trauma (specifically no bicycles and no trampolines). When can a patient be discharged home from the Emergency Department? The following criteria should be met: (a) Diagnosis is unequivocal and there is no active bleeding. (b) The child is well, and social circumstances allow confidence about the degree of parental supervision. (c) Safety in the home environment with respect to the risk of trauma. (d) There is ample opportunity for parental reassurance and education in the Emergency Department. (e) There is appropriate follow up by a paediatrician. Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura DPMS No 3531 Page 2
